ideas.To be is to be perceived (esse est perci:
"**To be is to be perceived**" (*esse est percipi*) is the central and most famous thesis of George Berkeley's philosophy — the startling principle at the heart of his **idealism** (or, as he preferred, "immaterialism"). It asserts that the existence of sensible things consists *entirely* in their being perceived: an object, insofar as it is a collection of sensible qualities, has no existence apart from its being perceived by some mind. There is no unperceived material substance lurking behind our perceptions; to be, for such things, simply *is* to be perceived.

Berkeley arrives at this by pressing the empiricism of Locke to a radical conclusion. What do we ever actually perceive? Not "matter" itself, but only **ideas** — colors, shapes, sounds, tastes, textures. An apple, on analysis, is just a bundle of such sensible qualities (red, round, sweet, firm). But all these qualities, Berkeley argues, exist only *in* the mind that perceives them; a color or a sound cannot exist unperceived, any more than a pain can. Since the object is nothing but its sensible qualities, and these exist only in being perceived, the object itself exists only in being perceived. "The various sensations or ideas imprinted on the sense... cannot exist otherwise than in a mind perceiving them."

The obvious objection — does the tree in the quad cease to exist when no one looks at it? — Berkeley answers with **God**: all things are continually perceived by the infinite mind of God, and so remain in stable existence even when no finite mind observes them. Reality is thus a system of ideas sustained in minds, ultimately God's. Strange as it sounds, Berkeley argues this is closer to common sense than materialism, since it takes the perceived world as fully real.
ideas.There is no matter, only minds and ideas:
The denial of **matter** — the claim that there is no such thing as mind-independent material substance, but only **minds and their ideas** — is the core positive doctrine of Berkeley's **immaterialism**, and the conclusion toward which his whole philosophy drives. For Berkeley, reality consists of just two kinds of thing: **spirits** (active, perceiving minds, including God and finite souls) and the **ideas** they perceive. There is no third category of inert, unthinking material stuff existing outside all minds.

Berkeley's arguments against matter are ingenious. He contends, first, that the very notion of matter — of an object existing *unperceived*, outside any mind — is incoherent: we cannot even *conceive* of an unperceived object, for the moment we try, we are conceiving (perceiving in thought) it, and so it is not unperceived after all. Second, he attacks Locke's distinction between **primary qualities** (extension, figure, motion, supposedly really in matter) and **secondary qualities** (color, sound, taste, admittedly mind-dependent). Berkeley argues that the primary qualities are *just as mind-dependent* as the secondary — you cannot conceive of shape or extension without some color or sensible quality, and all are relative to the perceiver — so *all* qualities exist only in the mind, and there is nothing left for "matter" to be. Third, matter, being inert and unperceivable, is explanatorily useless and cannot even be the cause of our ideas.

What causes our vivid, orderly ideas of sense, then? Not lifeless matter, but an active **spirit** — ultimately **God**, who imprints ideas on our minds in the regular patterns we call the "laws of nature." Reality is a language of ideas spoken by God to finite minds. Only minds and ideas exist; matter is a philosophers' fiction, and a dangerous one, Berkeley thought, leading to atheism and skepticism.
ideas.Critique of abstract ideas:
Berkeley's **critique of abstract ideas** is a crucial and foundational part of his philosophy — a sustained attack, prominent in the Introduction to his *Principles of Human Knowledge*, on the doctrine of "abstract general ideas" that he found in Locke and the broader tradition. Berkeley regarded this doctrine as a fundamental error and a chief source of confusion in philosophy, and its refutation clears the ground for his immaterialism.

The target is the theory that the mind can form **abstract** ideas by separating out and considering in isolation qualities that never actually occur separately. On Locke's account, for instance, the mind forms the abstract general idea of a "triangle" that is neither equilateral nor scalene nor isosceles but somehow all and none of these at once, or the idea of "color" in general that is no particular color. Berkeley denies that we can do any such thing. Try as we might, he argues, we cannot form an idea of a triangle that is no particular kind of triangle, or of a man who is no particular height or color. **Every idea we can actually conceive is fully determinate and particular.** The supposed abstract ideas are psychologically impossible fictions.

Berkeley does not deny that we can *reason generally* — but he explains generality differently: a *particular* idea (say, of one specific triangle) can be made to **represent** or stand for all others of its kind, functioning as a general sign, without our ever forming a genuinely abstract idea. This nominalist account of generality is important because Berkeley thinks the illusory doctrine of abstraction is precisely what allows philosophers to believe in "matter" — to imagine that they can conceive of "existence" abstracted from "being perceived." Demolishing abstraction thus removes the main prop of materialism.

works.A Treatise Concerning the Principles of:
*A Treatise Concerning the Principles of Human Knowledge* (1710) is Berkeley's principal philosophical work and the fullest systematic statement of his **immaterialism** — the revolutionary doctrine that matter does not exist and that reality consists only of minds and their ideas. Published when Berkeley was just twenty-five, it presents, in rigorous form, one of the boldest and most ingeniously argued positions in the history of philosophy.

The work opens with a famous **Introduction** devoted to demolishing the doctrine of **abstract general ideas**, which Berkeley regards as the root of much philosophical error, including the belief in matter. He argues that the mind cannot in fact form such abstractions, and that all our ideas are particular. The main body of the *Treatise* then develops the central thesis. Berkeley argues that the objects of knowledge are **ideas**; that ideas exist only in a perceiving **mind**; and hence that sensible things, being collections of ideas, exist only insofar as they are perceived — *esse est percipi*, "**to be is to be perceived**." From this he mounts his case against the existence of mind-independent **matter**, dismantling Locke's distinction between primary and secondary qualities and arguing that the very concept of matter is incoherent and useless.

Reality, Berkeley concludes, consists solely of **spirits** (active minds) and the **ideas** they perceive, with the regular order of our sensory ideas caused and sustained by the infinite spirit of **God**. Berkeley presents this not as skepticism but as a defense of common sense and religion against the materialism he believed led to atheism. He also addresses objections and applies his principles to problems in science, mathematics, and theology. Provocative and tightly reasoned, the *Treatise* is a classic of empiricist and idealist philosophy.
works.Three Dialogues between Hylas and Philon:
The *Three Dialogues between Hylas and Philonous* (1713) is Berkeley's most accessible, elegant, and persuasive presentation of his **immaterialism** — a restatement of the doctrines of his earlier *Principles of Human Knowledge* in the more engaging and rhetorically effective form of a **dialogue**. Written to win over readers who had found the *Principles* strange or paradoxical, it is widely regarded as a masterpiece of philosophical dialogue, ranking with those of Plato and Hume.

The conversation is between two characters whose Greek names signal their positions: **Hylas** (from *hyle*, "matter") defends the common belief in mind-independent material substance, while **Philonous** ("lover of mind") argues Berkeley's own view. Over three days of debate, Philonous skillfully leads Hylas, step by step, to abandon his materialism. The **first dialogue** argues that all sensible qualities — beginning with the secondary qualities (heat, taste, color, sound) and extending to the primary ones (extension, figure, motion) — are mind-dependent, existing only as they are perceived. The **second** presses the attack on the concept of material substance and introduces the role of God as the cause of our ideas. The **third** answers objections and shows that immaterialism, far from being the skeptical or absurd doctrine it seems, actually accords with common sense and secures the reality of the world.

A central and clever strategy of the *Dialogues* is Berkeley's insistence that it is the **materialist**, not he, who leads to **skepticism**: by positing an unknowable matter behind our perceptions, the materialist opens a gap between appearance and reality that his own view closes, since for Berkeley the sensible world we perceive *is* the real world. Charming, rigorous, and psychologically astute, the *Three Dialogues* remain the best entry point to Berkeley's thought.
